response_parsed.topic: What is the gender gap in science? Should we address it? And how?
response_parsed.opinion:
As a woman who's lived through various stages of life and work, I believe there's no doubt that a gender gap exists in science fields. Throughout my career path, from cleaning to teaching English part-time while raising one child alone, I've seen firsthand how many doors are closed because of someone's lack of confidence or perceived limitations.

The numbers don't lie - women make up only about 30% of the global science and engineering workforce even today. In academic positions, that number drops further to just around 20-25%. I remember seeing my own daughter discouraged when she wanted to join a robotics club at school because some teachers thought girls weren't suited for technical subjects.

This gap isn't solely about women being underrepresented in STEM; it's also about men not dominating all aspects of science. We need to address this carefully, focusing on equity rather than quotas. Creating mentorship opportunities and inclusive environments would help both genders feel comfortable contributing their talents. My own experiences with anxiety make me understand that self-doubt can be a barrier for anyone in any field.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: First, I considered my background as someone who has worked part-time throughout life - this gave me perspective on career interruptions and opportunities. Then, reflecting on the fact that both of my parents have STEM backgrounds (one with no formal education, one with PhD) made me think about how educational environment affects career choices. The personality traits helped too: moderate extraversion means I can engage others but still relate to introverted researchers' concerns; high agreeableness makes me sensitive to fairness issues; low neuroticism helps balance the anxiety aspect without being overwhelmed by it.
